content:
First note that if \(n\) has any digit zero then the left‐hand side (the product of the digits) is 0, while for \(n\ge17\) the right‐hand side
\[
n^2-15n-27>0,
\]
so no solution can have a zero digit.  Moreover for a \(d\)‐digit number with \(d\ge3\), the maximum digit‐product is \(9^d\), which grows much more slowly than \(n^2\), and one checks that already for \(n\ge37\),
\[
n^2-15n-27>9^3=729,
\]
so there can be no three‐digit (or longer) solutions.  Thus we need only check \(1\le n\le99\), excluding any with a zero digit.  

Checking one‐digit \(n\) or \(10\le n\le16\) yields no solutions.  For two‐digit \(n=10a+b\) with \(1\le a,b\le9\) the equation becomes
\[
ab \;=\;(10a+b)^2-15(10a+b)-27,
\]
which reduces to the quadratic in \(b\)
\[
b^2+(19a-15)b+(100a^2-150a-27)=0.
\]
Its discriminant is 
\[
\Delta=(19a-15)^2-4(100a^2-150a-27)
=-39a^2+30a+333,
\]
and one checks that the only \(1\le a\le9\) making \(\Delta\) a nonnegative perfect square is \(a=1\), giving \(\Delta=324\).  Then
\[
b=\frac{-(19\cdot1-15)\pm18}2
=\frac{-4\pm18}2
\]
and the only digit solution is \(b=7\).  Hence the unique solution is
\[
n=17.
\]
Therefore the sum of all positive integer solutions is
\[
\boxed{17}.
\]

\[
n^2-15n-27>9^3=729,

Checking one‐digit \(n\) or \(10\le n\le16\) yields no solutions.  For two‐digit \(n=10a+b\) with \(1\le a,b\le9\) the equation becomes
\[

\]

\[

\]
Its discriminant is 
\[
\Delta=(19a-15)^2-4(100a^2-150a-27)

\[
b=\frac{-(19\cdot1-15)\pm18}2

\[
